content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

February 23, 1996

Greg Schmidt
Executive Officer
Senate Rules Committee
California Legislature
State Capitol, Fourth Floor
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-055

Dear Mr. Schmidt:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Senator Hilda Solis regarding the "gift" and "contribution"
provisions under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. Will any of the public officials, public agencies, or
other persons who participate in a public meeting sponsored by
Senator Solis and a city council in her district, which will be
convened to discuss the impact of a state commission decision on
residents and businesses in her district, have made a gift or
campaign contribution to the Senator?

2. Will a public utility have made a gift or campaign
contribution to Senator Solis if the Senator includes
informational material developed by the utility in information
packets she sends her constituents, or if she incorporates the
information contained in the material in any response to a
constituent inquiry?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-96-055
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. The public officials, public agencies, or other persons
who participate in the public meeting will not have made a gift or
campaign contribution to Senator Solis.

2. The public utility will not have made a gift or campaign
contribution to Senator Solis.

FACTS²

Public Meeting

Senator Solis would like to invite a city council located in
her district to co-sponsor with her an information workshop (i.e.,
a "public meeting"). At issue is a recent decision by a state
commission which may or may not adversely affect residents and
businesses in the city. According to statute, interested parties
and state legislators have a limited time in which to comment on
the commission's actions. Senator Solis would like to conduct the
information workshop in the city council chamber. Legislative and
city personnel will attend the workshop and legislative and city
resources will be expended for the conduct and practical
management of the workshop.

Senator Solis would also like to invite a cross-section of
interested persons to appear at the workshop as panelists. These
interested persons would include a utility representative, a state
commission representative, a not-for-profit consumer advocacy
representative, and a regional government representative. Some of
these interested persons would incur travel expenses in order to
attend and would likely be reimbursed for those expenses by their
respective employers.

Utility Information Packets

Senator Solis and other elected officials in the Los Angeles
area have received printed material prepared by a public utility.
At issue is a recent decision by a regional water quality agency
and its effects on Senator Solis' constituents. Considerable
staff and resources were used by the utility in compilation of the
data contained in the material. The data was not compiled at the
Senator's request.

APPLICABLE LAW

Section 82015 defines "contribution" as any payment,
forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that

_____________
2 The facts include information provided in your letter dated
February 6, 1996, and information provided by Bob Leidigh, Senate
Rules Committee counsel, on February 15, 1996.

Our File No. A-96-055
Page 3


full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes. A payment is made for political purposes if
it is: (1) for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure; or (2) received by or made at the behest
of a candidate.3 (Regulation 18215(a).)

Section 82007 defines "candidate," in pertinent part, as any
individual who is listed on the ballot for elective office. An
individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her status
as a candidate until such time as that status is terminated
pursuant to Section 84214. Thus, as an elected official, Senator
Solis is considered a candidate under Section 82007.
Consequently, any payments received by or bequested by4 Senator
Solis are presumed to be for political purposes and will be
considered contributions absent an exception. (Danner Advice
Letter, A-96-056.)

Regulation 18215 provides in pertinent part:

(b) The term "contribution" includes:

* * * *

(3) Any goods or services received by or
bequested by a candidate or committee at no charge
or at a discount from the fair market value, unless
the discount is given in the regular course of
business to members of the public.


3  Regulation 18215 provides: "A payment is made for political
purposes if it is: (1) for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure; or (2) received by or
made at the behest of the following or any agent thereof: (A) a
candidate; (B) a controlled committee; (C) an official committee
of a political party, including a state central committee, county
central committee, assembly district committee or any subcommittee
of such committee; or (D) an organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes, including, but not limited to, a
political action committee established by any membership
organization, labor union or corporation.

4  A candidate "bequests" a contribution when the contribution is
made "under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or
suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of."
(Regulation 18225.7(a).)

Our File No. A-96-055
Page 4


     (c) Notwithstanding any other provision of
this section, the term "contribution" does not
include:

                         * * * *

     (3) A payment made by an occupant of a home or
office for costs related to any meeting or
fundraising event held in the occupant's home or
office, if the total cost of the meeting or
fundraising event is $500 or less, exclusive of the
fair rental value of the premises.

     Where a payment is not a contribution, it may in some
circumstances be considered a gift. "Gift" is defined in Section
82028 as any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or
greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in
the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status.

     The term "gift" does not include material which serves
primarily to convey information and which is provided for the
purpose of assisting the recipient in the performance of his or
her official duties. (Section 82028; Regulation 18942.1.) Of
course, the determination of whether a payment is a gift or
contribution is a factual question which must be determined on a
case-by-case basis.

                        ANALYSIS

Contributions

     a.  Public Meeting

     The public officials, public agencies, or other persons who
participate in the public meeting will not have made a gift or a
campaign contribution to Senator Solis as discussed below.

     According to the information you have provided, Senator Solis
would like to organize a public meeting at a city council chamber
in her district. Thus, payments made at the behest of Senator
Solis in connection with the public meeting will be considered
contributions to the Senator absent an exception. Moreover, she
would like a city council to co-sponsor the event. Thus, any
payments bestowed by the officials will be considered contributions
to both officials, absent an exception.

     The city chamber would be made available to Senator Solis by
the city at her request, presumably at no cost. We believe the
use of the chamber is analogous to a payment made by an occupant
of an office for costs related to a meeting held in the occupant's
office. Therefore, we would apply the exception in Regulation
18215(c)(3). The city chamber will be used for a meeting as

Our File No. A-96-055
Page 5


contemplated by the regulation. In addition, the chamber itself
is the "office" of the city council for purposes of applying the
exclusion to a governmental entity. Provided the costs of the
meeting do not exceed $500, exclusive of the fair rental value of
the premises, we conclude this exception applies.5

You have also asked whether the legislative and city
personnel who will attend and conduct the physical management of
the workshop would be making a gift or contribution to the
Senator. This would be in the normal course of their employment.
Pursuant to Regulation 18420(b), the payment by a state or local
government agency of the salary or expenses of its employees or
agents is an expenditure or contribution only if the salary or
expenses are for campaign activities and meet the requirements of
Regulation 18423. The activities described are not "campaign
activities" within meaning of Regulation 18420. Therefore, no
contribution would result from the use of legislative and city
personnel to conduct the public meeting.

You also stated that several individuals will be invited to
make presentations at the meeting, including a utility
representative, a state water commission representative, a not-for-
profit consumer advocacy representative, and a regional government
representative. You have indicated that some of these individuals
will be asking their employers for reimbursement of their travel
expenses. Therefore, presumably, these participants will be
providing the presentation in the course of their employment.

The same analysis would apply to the presenters who are
public employees as that discussed above with respect to the
legislative staff and city personnel. As to other presenters,
Regulation 18423 provides an exception from the definition of
"contribution" where an individual is paid by his or her employer
and the individual spends less than 10% of his or her compensated
time in a calendar month rendering services for political
purposes. The exception would include reimbursement for personal
expenses, such as travel, paid for by an employer. (Regulation
18423(d).) Based on your facts, the personal services would not
be rendered for political purposes, as defined in Regulation
18423(b).6


5 Please note that expenses which are neither a gift nor a
contribution pursuant to a separate statutory or regulatory
exception would not be considered in calculating the total costs
of the event for purposes of applying this exception.

6 For purposes of Regulation 18423, personal services are
rendered for "political purposes" if they are carried on for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of
the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or
more candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18423(b).)

Our File No. A-96-055
Page 6


     b.  Utility Information Packets

     You have also asked if utility company material received by,
but not bequested by, the Senator, would be considered a gift or
contribution. The public utility will not have made a campaign
contribution to Senator Solis by distributing informational
material to her as discussed below.

     You state that considerable utility staff and resources were
used to compile the information. We would apply the same analysis
as discussed above with respect to the staff resources.
Therefore, the dissemination of the information packets received
by the Senator from a public utility would not constitute a
contribution to the Senator. Moreover, the exception in
Regulation 18215(c) for goods or services given in the regular
course of business to members of the public appears to apply.

Gifts

     We have determined that no contribution would result from the
personal services donated. We address whether the services of
these presenters would constitute a gift to the Senator instead.
(Section 82028.)

     According to your facts, the services will primarily convey
information and assist the State Senator in the performance of her
official duties or of her elective office. Therefore, the donated
personal services would be considered "informational material" and
not gifts to the State Senator. (Regulation 18942.1.) Since no
gift to the State Senator would result, the travel to the
presenters paid for by their employers would, similarly, under
these facts, not constitute gifts.

     I trust this letter addresses your concerns. If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [illegible]

     By:  Luisa Menchaca
          Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LM:ak
